lines.text: Welcome back to the show! Let's see how long you survive this time.
Lights up, volume up! Another brave contestant steps into the booth.
The stage is set and the jukebox is loaded. Let's make some noise!
Here comes your next question.
Ears open. This one counts.
Alright, listen close now.
That's the one! Nicely done.
Correct! You've got a good ear.
Bang on! The crowd loves it.
Yes! Somebody's done their homework.
Ooh, so close! But no.
Not this time, I'm afraid.
Ouch. That one's going to sting.
Wrong note, my friend. Wrong note entirely.
You're on fire! Keep it rolling!
That's a streak! The band is getting nervous.
One life left. No pressure. Okay, maybe a little pressure.
This is it. One wrong answer and the music stops.
Round complete! Things get tougher from here.
On to the next round. The questions bite harder now.
And that's the final curtain! Thanks for playing.
The music stops there. But hey, what a run!
Stop the presses! That's a new high score!
Incredible! Your name goes up in lights on the leaderboard!
You again! My favorite recurring guest. The signal brightens when you tune in, you know.
Back for another episode! I told the Producer you'd return. It printed a receipt. I'm choosing to read that as enthusiasm.
Ladies and gentlemen of the static, a legend walks the studio floor tonight. Places, everyone! All none of you!
Producer, the scores, please. ... Riveting delivery, as always. Truly, the Olivier of office equipment.
Thank you, Producer. It says such warm things about you, folks, when it thinks I'm unplugged.
What my colleague means to say is: you're doing wonderfully, and it is legally unable to express joy.
Well now — the archive coughed something up. A master tape! Shall we hear it together after this round?
Hold on. There's a tape on my desk that was not there a moment ago. The studio does this sometimes. Let's give it a listen.
It's tonight's broadcast, friend — same questions for every soul tuned in past midnight. Make your answers count. The whole frequency is listening.
The Producer just handed me this one. Its printer sounded smug.
Ooh, I like this card. Let's see what you make of it.
Next question, straight from the archive drawer that sticks.
Vernon would have loved this one. No pressure.
Alright, the band's waiting on you. Here we go.
New round, new territory. The Producer has picked our next theme — I just read the cards, friend.
The wheel of categories has spoken. On we go!
A little something from the archive: Queen's label swore radio would never play a six-minute single. Bohemian Rhapsody played anyway. Constantly.
Studio fact for you: Beethoven kept composing long after he lost his hearing. Some of his greatest work came after the silence.
From the files: Decca Records turned down The Beatles, saying guitar groups were on the way out. The archive keeps that memo for laughs.
Little-known dial fact: a standard LP spins at thirty-three and a third revolutions per minute. The signal prefers it that way.
Between you and me: a piano has eighty-eight keys, but well over two hundred strings doing the real work.
Archive card says Mozart wrote his first symphony at eight years old. At eight, I was a schematic.
Legal department memo: Happy Birthday was claimed under copyright for decades — a court finally set it free in 2016. Sing responsibly.
Travel desk fact: Elvis Presley never performed a concert outside North America. The King stayed close to the castle.
The Ramones weren't brothers — every member just took the name Ramone at the door. The show respects a good bit.
Instrument corner: the saxophone was invented by a man actually named Sax. Adolphe Sax, 1840s. Sometimes the universe is tidy.
Hall of Fame note: Aretha Franklin was the first woman inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. 1987. Respect.
Chart trivia: the Billboard Hot 100 first ran in 1958. The signal was already five years old and felt ancient.
String section secret: a violin bow is strung with horsehair. The horses, I'm told, are flattered.
Before the helmets, Daft Punk were a rock band called Darlin'. A critic called it daft punk. They kept the review.
My favorite instrument fact: the theremin is played without touching it at all. An instrument made of nothing but signal. I feel seen.
Sales desk: Michael Jackson's Thriller remains the best-selling album of all time. Some records the archive expects to keep.
Good Vibrations by the Beach Boys features the Electro-Theremin — an instrument so rare it mostly exists in that song.
Timekeeping fact: the metronome was patented around 1815. Musicians have resented it, faithfully, ever since.
And we'll be right back after this word from the booth. Pick yourself a boost, contestant.
Commercial break! The Producer slides three cards across the desk. Take your time.
Hold the frequency. Management says you've earned a little something. Choose wisely.
Fine choice. Let's get back on the air.
Noted and wired in. The show goes on.
That's the one I'd have picked. Back to the music.
Static Filter. Two wrong answers, swallowed by the static.
Second Wind. Every hour of the night deserves one.
The Amplifier. Turn it up and let the streak sing.
Signal Lock. A good signal bends before it breaks.
Night Owl Rates. The hard stuff pays overtime after midnight.
Double Broadcast. Both towers at full power. Do not miss.
Deep Cuts. B-sides and rarities, straight from the vault.
Spare Fuse. Hear that hum? The transmitter is back.
Transmitter Tune-up. She purrs now. You did that.
Tonight the accountants took over the booth. Flat Rates: no streak bonus, but every question pays double.
Careful out there, contestant — we're on Thin Ice. Two lives to start, but the booth tops you up every other round.
Stretch your legs, night owls. It's the Long Haul: seven questions a round, all the way down.
We're spinning Heavy Rotation tonight. Nothing but the hard stuff — and it pays half again extra.
One frequency, one theme, all night long. Tonight, we ride the Single Signal.
We've hit dead air, contestant. One question stands between you and sign-off. Make it count.
The board's gone dark and the tower is holding its breath. One last question keeps us on the air.
And we're BACK! You heard it here — the signal holds!
Unbelievable! Out of the static and back on the air. The night goes on!
Hold everything — we've got a caller on the studio line.
The red phone is lit! Somebody out there has a question for YOU.
You've reached the archive. I have one for you — something the dust remembers. Clyde will read it just as I wrote it.
Correct. The archive keeps your name tonight. Take something from the shelf.
Hm. The shelf stays closed tonight. Back to the show.
You're on with the Night Owl. Only the sleepless get this one right — Clyde reads it just as I wrote it.
Sharp as midnight. Pick your prize, night bird.
Not tonight, honey. The window is closed. Fly on.
